coredata.dc:title: ‘On-off-on’ electrochemiluminescent aptasensor for Hg2+ based on dual signal amplification enabled by a self-enhanced luminophore and resonance energy transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Libo
Chen, Bainian
Liu, Xiaohong
Jiang, Panao
Luo, Lijun
Li, Xia
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: Divalent mercury ion (Hg2+) is widely distributed in the ecological environment and harmful to the human body and the environment. Herein, a highly sensitive and selective ‘on–off-on’ electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptamer sensing method based on the dual signal amplification strategy of a self-enhanced luminophore and ECL resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) was developed to detect Hg2+. In detail, the first ‘switch-on’ state was obtained by the self-enhanced luminophore of Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticle-nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (Ru@SiO2-NGQDs) modified glassy carbon electrode, which significantly amplified the ECL intensity compared with the mixture of Ru@SiO2 and NGQDs. Later, the ‘switch-off’ state was induced by gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), which acted the acceptor in the ECL-RET system between AuNPs and Ru@SiO2-NGQDs due to their good spectral overlap. This combination of the self-enhanced luminophore and ECL-RET provided a novel dual signal amplification strategy, which led to a high signal-to-noise ratio and high analysis sensitivity. Finally, upon the addition of Hg2+, the formation of a stable T-Hg2+-T structure induced the release of AuNPs from the sensing interface and halted the ECL-RET process, resulting in ECL signal recovery (the second ‘switch-on’ state). For the detection of Hg2+, the proposed ECL sensor exhibited a wide linear range from 1 × 10−14 to 1 × 10−6 M and a highly sensitive detection limit of 3.0 fM. Finally, the feasibility of the developed method in water sample analysis was investigated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Dual signal amplification
Self-enhanced
Resonance energy transfer
Hg2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of cDNA-modified self-enhanced ECL luminophore
Fabrication of the ‘on–off-on’ ECL aptasensor and ECL measurement
Feasibility investigation and fabrication of the ECL aptasensor
Analytical performance investigation of the ‘on–off-on’ ECL aptasensor for Hg2+ detection
Practical application of the proposed method for Hg2+detection in water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A-C) TEM images of Ru@SiO2, Ru@SiO2-NH2 and Ru@SiO2-NGQDs, respectively. Insets are the schematic diagrams of each material. (D) Fluorescence spectra of (a) Ru@SiO2, (b) Ru@SiO2-NH2 (c) Ru@SiO2-NGQDs and (d) NGQDs.

(A) Agarose gel electrophoresis images of (a) cDNA, (b) Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA and (c) Ru@SiO2-NGQDs. (B) UV–Vis absorbance spectra and (C) XPS spectra of (a) Ru@SiO2-NGQDs, (b) cDNA and (c) Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA.

(A) TEM image and (B) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of AuNPs. Inset of (A): particle size distribution of AuNPs. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of (a) AuNPs-Apt and (b) Apt. (D) XPS spectra of (a) AuNPs, (b) Apt and (c) AuNPs-Apt.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the AuNPs and ECL spectrum of the Ru@SiO2-NGQDs. (B) CV and (C) EIS of (a) bare GCE, (b) Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA/GCE, (c) AuNPs-Apt/Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA/GCE and (d) BSA/AuNPs-Apt/Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA/GCE in 10 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl. (D) ECL responses of (a) Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA/GCE, (b) AuNPs-Apt/Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA/GCE, (c) BSA/AuNPs-Apt/Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA/GCE and (d-f) BSA/AuNPs-Apt/Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA/GCE incubated with 1 × 10−8, 1 × 10−7 and 1 × 10−6 M Hg2+, respectively. ECL measurement solution: 0.1 M, pH 7.5 PBS.

(A) ECL-time curves of the proposed ECL aptasensor in the absence and presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ from 1 × 10−14 to 1 × 10−6 M. Inset: calibration curve of ECL intensity versus Hg2+ concentration. (B) Change in ECL intensity of the aptasensor before and after incubation with different metal ions. (C) Repeatability and (D) stability investigation of the aptasensor.
Fabrication of the ‘on–off-on’ ECL aptasensor for Hg2+ detection enabled by the dual signal amplification strategy of a self-enhanced luminophore and resonance energy transfer.
Comparison of the linear range and LOD of the proposed method with previous ECL sensors based on different signal output modes.

head.ce:title: ‘On-off-on’ electrochemiluminescent aptasensor for Hg2+ based on dual signal amplification enabled by a self-enhanced luminophore and resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong 252059, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong 252059, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Divalent mercury ion (Hg2+) is widely distributed in the ecological environment and harmful to the human body and the environment. Herein, a highly sensitive and selective ‘on–off-on’ electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptamer sensing method based on the dual signal amplification strategy of a self-enhanced luminophore and ECL resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) was developed to detect Hg2+. In detail, the first ‘switch-on’ state was obtained by the self-enhanced luminophore of Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticle-nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (Ru@SiO2-NGQDs) modified glassy carbon electrode, which significantly amplified the ECL intensity compared with the mixture of Ru@SiO2 and NGQDs. Later, the ‘switch-off’ state was induced by gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), which acted the acceptor in the ECL-RET system between AuNPs and Ru@SiO2-NGQDs due to their good spectral overlap. This combination of the self-enhanced luminophore and ECL-RET provided a novel dual signal amplification strategy, which led to a high signal-to-noise ratio and high analysis sensitivity. Finally, upon the addition of Hg2+, the formation of a stable T-Hg2+-T structure induced the release of AuNPs from the sensing interface and halted the ECL-RET process, resulting in ECL signal recovery (the second ‘switch-on’ state). For the detection of Hg2+, the proposed ECL sensor exhibited a wide linear range from 1 × 10−14 to 1 × 10−6 M and a highly sensitive detection limit of 3.0 fM. Finally, the feasibility of the developed method in water sample analysis was investigated.

ce:section.ce:para: Along with the relentless increase in industrialisation, agricultural modernisation and urbanisation, large amounts of heavy metal pollutants have entered the environment through the excessive discharge of the ‘three wastes’, as well as irresponsible dumping of domestic waste and indiscriminate use of pesticide and fertiliser. This has led to serious pollution of soil, water and atmosphere [1]. Mercury is one of the most widespread and toxic heavy metal pollutants, and bivalent inorganic mercury ion (Hg2+) is its main and most stable form [1]. In the ecological environment, Hg2+ may inhibit the growth and development of crops, resulting in decreased crop yields. Additionally, Hg2+ can bioaccumulate in the food chain, especially aquatic seafood. Humans, at the top of the food chain, are exposed to the greatest hazard, and various diseases can arise even at extremely low Hg2+ concentrations [2]. Therefore, it is still crucial to develop a high-performance method for Hg2+ analysis.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensors, which can offer high sensitivity and low background noise due to the advantages of ECL technique and excellent specificity from the aptamer of the target, have been widely fabricated for the detection of Hg2+. In general, the fundamental mechanism underpinning these Hg2+ ECL aptasensors is that Hg2+ can specifically bind with thymine (T)-rich aptamer to form more stable T-Hg2+-T complexes [3]. According to the ECL signal change with increasing concentrations of Hg2+, ECL Hg2+ aptasensors have been classified into three signal output modes including ‘signal off’ [4–6], ‘signal on’ [7–9] and ‘on–off-on’ [10–12]. Among these, ‘on–off-on’-type sensors exhibit lower background noise and higher sensitivity than the ‘signal off’ sensors and more specificity than the ‘signal on’ sensors [13].

Generally, in ‘on–off-on’ switch system, high sensitivity can be achieved by obtaining the highest initial ‘switch-on’ signal and the lowest following ‘switch-off’ signal [14]. To enhance the first ‘switch-on’ state, signal amplification strategies have been widely designed, mainly including signal amplification assisted by biotechnology and signal amplification via ECL system. Compared to bio-assisted signal amplification methods such as DNA-based and enzyme-based signal amplification strategies, the second type of signal amplification is more competitive due to the low cost, simple preparation procedure and better stability of ECL materials than DNA and enzyme [13,14]. Considering from the perspective of ECL luminophore and co-reactant, respectively, various signal amplification strategies of ECL system have been widely developed. For example, since the first report of Pt(II) complexes with aggregation-induced ECL (AIECL) performance [15], diverse types of ECL luminophores including Ir-based complexes, small molecules, polymeric dots and metal nanocrystals or nanoparticles have been prepared [16,17]. Compared with traditional ECL materials, these luminophores generally exhibit higher ECL efficiencies in their aggregated states than that of the typical Ru(bpy)3 2+/tripropylamine system [17,18]. However, the practical application of AIECL-based luminophores in analytical sensing filed is still in its infancy.

Signal amplification and further sensitivity enhancement for ECL aptasensors can also be achieved by focusing on the co-reactant, such as exploring ideal co-reactants [19,20], developing novel co-reaction accelerators [21–23] and synthesising high-performance self-enhanced luminophores [24,25]. Design and preparation of novel self-enhanced ECL emitters has emerged as a powerful signal amplification strategy due to their characteristic features of the shortened electron-transfer distance and reduced energy loss, benefiting from the coexistence of a luminophore and co-reactant in one molecular or nanostructure [24]. In our preliminary works, we reported a novel self-enhanced luminophore by electrostatically binding Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (Ru@SiO2 NPs) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) [26] due to the excellent co-reactant performance of NGQDs [27]. Based on its outstanding ECL performance, a self-enhanced ECL aptasensor was fabricated to detect Hg2+ at the picomolar level. However, further development such as signal amplification strategy should be adopted to improve the ECL performance [28].

The other important aspect affecting the sensitivity of ‘on–off-on’ switch systems is the establishment of the ‘switch-off’ state to obtain a low background signal. This can be achieved by the introduction of ECL quenchers [13]. Until now, the quenching mechanisms of ECL systems have been divided into benzoquinone-type quenching, electron-transfer quenching, carbon-based nanomaterials quenching, resonance energy transfer (RET) quenching, etc [29]. Among these, ECL resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) systems involve a process of nonradiative energy transfer from the excited-state ECL luminophore to an energy acceptor when they exhibit satisfactory spectral overlap at a suitable distance [30,31]. Benefiting from high sensitivity, good specificity, wide applicability and easy operation, the RET protocol has also been utilised as a novel signal amplification strategy combined with ECL or the photoelectrochemistry signal-out mode [32,33]. In recent years, Au-based nanomaterials including Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) [34], Au nanorods (AuNRs) [35], Au nanocages (AuNCs) [36] and Au nanodendrites (AuNDs) [37] have been widely applied as energy acceptors in ECL-RET systems due to their programmable absorption spectra, which can be controlled by adjusting their size and shape [30]. For Hg2+ detection, Babamiri et al. developed an ultra-sensitive and specific aptasensor based on the highly efficient ECL-RET between CdTe@CdS quantum dots and AuNPs, which could detect Hg2+ at the attomolar level [11].

Inspired by these concepts, a sensitive and selective ‘on–off-on’ switch ECL aptasensor for Hg2+ analysis was developed based on the dual signal amplification strategy with a self-enhanced luminophore and ECL-RET. As shown in Scheme 1 , self-enhanced ECL luminophore Ru@SiO2-NGQDs, which showed significantly amplified ECL intensity compared with the control group, a mixture of Ru@SiO2 and NGQDs [26], was assembled on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface to obtain the first ‘switch-on’ state. Then, AuNPs were introduced through the hybridisation reaction between Hg2+ aptamer (Apt) and complementary DNA (cDNA), linked with AuNPs and Ru@SiO2-NGQDs, respectively, to form a novel ECL-RET system and achieve the ‘switch-off’ state. Finally, with the addition of Hg2+, a stable T-Hg2+-T structure was generated. This released the energy acceptors (AuNPs) from the sensing interface and prevented the ECL-RET process, thereby recovering the ECL intensity and achieving the second ‘switch-on’ state. Profiting from the dual amplification by the self-enhanced luminophore and ECL-RET, the proposed ECL aptasensor was successfully applied to detect Hg2+ with a highly sensitive detection limit of 3.0 fM.

Ammonium citrate (A.R.), sodium citrate (A.R.), Triton X-100 (TX-100, A.R.), cyclohexane (99%), n-hexanol (GC), ammonium hydroxide (25%), ethanol (A.R.), acetone (A.R.), disodium phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O, A.R.) and sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O, A.R.) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) dichlororuthenium(II) hexahydrate (Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O, 98%), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 98%) and bovine serum albumin (BSA, A.R.) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98%), gold(III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, A.R.) and mercury nitrate dihydrate (Hg(NO3)2·2H2O, 97%) were provided by Aladdin (China), Acros Organics (USA) and Tongren Chemical Reagents (China), respectively. Agarose (Solarbio), 50 bp DNA ladder (Solarbio), TAE (50×, pH = 8.0, Solarbio), 6 × loading buffer (Solarbio) and gel green (Nanjing Genesis Technology Co., Ltd.) were used for gel electrophoresis experiments. The sulfhydryl-functionalised aptamer of Hg2+ (Apt) and amino-functionalised complementary DNA (cDNA) used in the present work were prepared by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (China), and their sequences were as follows:
Apt: 5′-TTG TTT GTC CCC TCT TTC TTA-(CH2)3-SH-3′
cDNA: 5′-TAA GAA AGA GGG GAC AAA-(CH2)3-NH2-3′

A Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, USA) instrument was used for transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which was used to image the nanomaterials prepared in the present work. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) adsorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained using a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an Escalab 250 Xi (Thermo Fisher, USA). Gel electrophoresis images were obtained using a gel imaging analyser (Tanon, China). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed on an Autolab 144 PGSTAT 302 N EC workstation (Metrohm, Netherlands). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) data and ECL intensity were collected on an MPI-EII ECL analyser (Xi'an Remax, China). For all the ECL and CV tests, a modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm), a platinum wire electrode and an Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl) electrode comprised the traditional three-electrode system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cDNA-modified self-enhanced ECL luminophore
ce:section.ce:para:
The self-enhanced ECL luminophore, named Ru@SiO2-NGQDs, was prepared according to our previously developed method as shown in the Supplementary Material
s [26,28]. Then, the cDNA-modified Ru@SiO2-NGQDs (Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA) were synthesised via a condensation reaction between the carboxyl (–COOH) on the NGQDs surface and amino groups (–NH2) at the 3′ end of cDNA. The specific steps were as follows. The as-prepared Ru@SiO2-NGQDs (1.6 mL) were mixed with phosphate buffer solution (PBS; pH 7.4, 10 mM, 0.8 mL) containing EDC (10 mM) and NHS (5 mM). Activation of the –COOH groups was achieved by stirring the mixture for 15 min. Upon the addition of cDNA (3 µM, 1.6 mL), the condensation reaction was triggered and was complete after stirring the system for 2 h. The obtained faint yellow solution was collected and sorted at 4 ℃ for future use.

Pure AuNPs were prepared via the Turkevich method with slight modification [38]. HAuCl4·3H2O (0.1 M, 0.2 mL) was mixed with ultrapure water (25 mL) in a clean three-necked flask and heated at 150 ℃. Sodium citrate (100 mg·mL−1, 0.25 mL) was immediately added and the obtained mixture was allowed to react for 15 min, at which time the solution was purple-red. The product was then cooled to room temperature and the concentration was adjusted to 5 nM by UV–Vis spectroscopy. The obtained AuNPs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for future use.

Hg2+ aptamer-modified AuNPs (AuNPs-Apt) were subsequently synthesised via the Au-S bond mechanism [39]. The obtained AuNPs solution (1.95 mL) and Hg2+ aptamer (100 μM, 0.05 mL) were mixed. After standing for 1 h in the dark, the solution was stirred for 16 h to generate the AuNPs-Apt. The mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove insoluble impurities and the supernatant was stored at 4 ℃ for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the ‘on–off-on’ ECL aptasensor and ECL measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication process for the ‘on–off-on’ ECL sensor is shown in Scheme 1. Prior to modification, the bare GCE (3 mm) was successively polished with 0.3 μm and 0.05 μm alumina slurry to remove surface impurities, followed by ultrasonic cleaning in ultrapure water, ethanol, acetone and ultrapure water, sequentially. Subsequently, Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA solution (6 μL) was dropped onto the cleaned GCE surface and dried at 37 ℃ to obtain Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA/GCE. Next, AuNPs-Apt solution (6 μL) was cast on the Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA/GCE to construct the ECL-RET system via hybridisation between cDNA and Hg2+ aptamer at 37 ℃ for 70 min. The resulting product was named AuNPs-Apt/Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA/GCE. Finally, to avoid the nonspecific binding effect, the resultant electrode was immersed into BSA (1 wt%, 0.08 mL) and incubated at room temperature for 1 h. Additionally, after each modification, the electrode was thoroughly washed with PBS (pH 7.5, 0.1 M).

For the detection of Hg2+, the prepared ECL aptasensor was immersed into different concentrations of Hg2+ solution and incubated for 80 min. The ECL intensity of the resultant aptasensor was recorded on the MPI-EII ECL analyser in PBS (pH 7.5, 0.1 M). The potential was scanned from 0.2 to 1.25 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) with a scan rate of 0.1 V·s−1 and a photomultiplier tube voltage of 800 V.

Water samples, including tap water from Jiangsu University and river water from the Beijing-Hangzhou Grand Canal (Zhenjiang reach), were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove large impurities, and then filtered through a membrane (0.22 μm). The resulting samples were diluted with PBS (pH 7.5, 0.1 M) and spiked with different concentrations of Hg2+ for analysis using the developed sensor.

To verify the successful synthesis of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs, TEM and fluorescence spectroscopy were conducted and corresponding results are shown in Fig. 1 . Upon the encapsulation of Ru(bpy)3 2+ into the SiO2 NPs, the obtained Ru@SiO2 NPs maintained a monodisperse spherical structure with a uniform particle size distribution and an average diameter of 42.3 nm (Fig. 1A). To confirm the electrostatic adsorption of NGQDs (characterisation is shown in Fig. S1) by Ru@SiO2, the obtained Ru@SiO2 NPs were functionalised with APTES. Fig. 1B shows that the size distribution of Ru@SiO2-NH2 was still narrow with the average diameter almost unchanged (43.8 nm). Although the microscopic morphology of Ru(bpy)3 2+ in Ru@SiO2 and Ru@SiO2-NH2 could not be observed due to its molecular-type structure, the successful encapsulation of Ru(bpy)3 2+ into SiO2 NPs was confirmed by fluorescence spectroscopy, where both Ru@SiO2 and Ru@SiO2-NH2 exhibited the characteristic fluorescence emission of Ru(bpy)3 2+ at 633 nm (Fig. 1D, curves a and b) [40]. Furthermore, NGQDs was uniformly attached on the surface of Ru@SiO2-NH2 after the electrostatic interaction between them (Fig. 1C), and the average diameter of the obtained Ru@SiO2-NGQDs composite increased to 52.2 nm, preliminarily indicating the successful preparation of the self-enhanced ECL luminophore. In addition, as shown in Fig. 1D, except the fluorescence peak of Ru(bpy)3 2+, there was another fluorescence signal at 417 nm (curve c) corresponding to the NGQDs (curve d), which further confirmed the successful combination of Ru@SiO2-NH2 with NGQDs. Moreover, zeta potential experiments were conducted to verify the successful preparation of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs (Fig. S2). As displayed in Fig. S2, the electric charge of Ru@SiO2 changed from −10.9 mV (Column a) to 3.23 mV (Column b) after functionalized with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane to form Ru@SiO2-NH2. Upon the addition of negatively charged NGQDs (Column c, −13.2 mV), zeta potential of Ru@SiO2-NH2 further changed from positive to negative (Column d, −8.85 mV), demonstrating the successful preparation of the composite material through electrostatic interaction as our previous reported works [26,41].

The modification of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs with cDNA was monitored by agarose gel electrophoresis, UV–Vis absorbance spectroscopy and XPS. Fig. 2 A showed a distinct cDNA band with fast electrophoretic mobility (lane a) due to its small molecular weight [36]. However, after reacting with Ru@SiO2-NGQDs, no cDNA band appeared in lane b, indicating that most of the cDNA was successfully modified on the surface of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs to form Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA. This hindered the translational movement of cDNA through the hydrogel pores because of the increased mass of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs (lane c) [42].

As can be seen from Fig. 2B, three distinct absorption peaks of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs were observed at 286, 326 and 453 nm (curve a). The absorption peaks at 286 and 453 nm were attributed to the ligand-centred transitions and metal-to-ligand charge transfer of Ru(bpy)3 2+, respectively, and the peak at 326 nm was assigned to the π-π* conjugate structures of aromatic CC domains, illustrating the successful preparation of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs. An absorption peak of the cDNA strand at 265 nm (curve b) was also observed. After the amide reaction between the Ru@SiO2-NGQDs and cDNA, Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA displayed all the characteristic peaks of cDNA and Ru@SiO2-NGQDs (curve c), and the characteristic peak of the Ru@SiO2-NGQDs shifted from 286 to 279 nm. These results confirmed the successful modification of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs with cDNA.

Meanwhile, XPS results (Fig. 2C) for Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA (curve c) exhibited the P 2p binding energy peak at 133.33 eV. This was consistent with the presence of cDNA (curve b), also confirming the successful formation of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA.

From TEM, the mean diameter of the as-prepared AuNPs was measured to be 10.3 nm (Fig. 3 A) with a narrow particle size distribution range of 8.4–12.6 nm (inset of Fig. 3A). The characteristic AuNPs peak at 519 nm was observed in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3B), demonstrating their successful synthesis [38]. Upon reaction with the Hg2+ aptamer, the obtained AuNPs-Apt not only retained the characteristic absorption of AuNPs, with a 3 nm redshift, but also displayed a new peak at 260 nm (Fig. 3C, curve a), corresponding to the base in Apt (Fig. 3C, curve b) with a 7 nm blueshift after combination with the AuNPs [43].

XPS was also performed to characterise the AuNPs-Apt. As illustrated in Fig. 3D, the bare AuNPs exhibited three characteristic peaks related to Au 4f (83.74 eV), C 1 s (284.80 eV) and O 1 s (531.93 eV), as shown in curve a [44]. For the pure Apt, in addition to the C 1 s (284.80 eV) and O 1 s (531.47 eV) peaks, two peaks corresponding to P 2p and N 1 s were also observed at 133.52 eV and 400.19 eV, respectively (curve b). As expected, upon AuNPs modification by Apt, all the characteristic peaks, Au 4f (83.49 eV), P 2p (133.39 eV), C 1 s (284.80 eV), N 1 s (400.12 eV) and O 1 s (531.51 eV), were observed in the spectrum of the AuNPs-Apt composite (curve c). These results were verified by high-resolution XPS, which showed the Au 4f and P 2p signals in AuNPs, Apt and AuNPs-Apt (Fig. S3). These results adequately demonstrated the successful preparation of AuNPs-Apt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility investigation and fabrication of the ECL aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence and ECL spectra of the Ru@SiO2-NGQDs demonstrated that Ru@SiO2 played a role of luminophore, while NGQDs were utilized as the co-reactant (Fig. S4). Furthermore, the ECL spectrum of the Ru@SiO2-NGQDs and the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the AuNPs were compared to investigate the feasibility of ECL-RET from the Ru@SiO2-NGQDs to AuNPs. Fig. 4 A shows that the AuNPs had an obvious and intense UV–Vis absorption centred at 519 nm and good spectral overlap with the ECL spectrum of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs at 646 nm. This considerable spectral overlap indicated the possibility of ECL-RET between Ru@SiO2-NGQDs and AuNPs, which was further confirmed by ECL measurements. Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA/GCE exhibited a relatively strong ECL signal with an average intensity of 4888 a.u. (Fig. S5, curve a), which decreased about 70.0% upon the addition of AuNPs-Apt (curve c). As a control, the reduced ECL intensity value induced by Apt alone was excluded. Apt also decreased the ECL signal due to the poor conductivity of the biomolecule (curve b). However, the decrease in ECL caused by Apt (47.7%) was smaller than that caused by AuNPs-Apt (70.0%), suggesting that the AuNPs indeed served as energy transfer acceptor to fabricate the ECL-RET-based ECL aptasensor.

CV and EIS measurements were performed to monitor the fabrication of the ECL aptasensor. As shown in Fig. 4B, the bare GCE exhibited a pair of distinct and symmetrical redox peaks with high current intensity in 10 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl (curve a). However, upon successive modification with Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA, AuNPs-Apt and BSA, gradual decreases in the redox current were obtained (curves b-d); this was attributed to the poor conductivity of the biomolecules and the repulsive interaction between negatively-charged cDNA or Apt and the electrochemical probe of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−.

Changes in the electron-transfer resistance value (Ret) during the fabrication were also recorded. As shown in Fig. 4C, the successive assembly of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA (curve b), AuNPs-Apt (curve c) and BSA (curve d) on the bare GCE surface each increased the Ret value in comparison with the bare GCE (curve a). This was highly consistent with the results of the CV tests and indicated the successful construction of the aptasensor. In addition, agarose gel electrophoresis experiments demonstrated the successful hybridization of double-stranded DNA and the specific recognition of Hg2+ with its aptamer in the presence of Hg2+ (Fig. S6).

Finally, the performance of the ‘on–off-on’ switch ECL aptasensor based on the signal amplification of the self-enhanced luminophore and ECL-RET was investigated by recording the ECL responses after each step. Fig. 4D shows that Ru@SiO2-NGQDs-cDNA/GCE emitted a significantly strong ECL signal in 0.1 M pH 7.5 PBS without any co-reactant (curve a), profiting from the self-enhanced property of the Ru@SiO2-NGQDs. On this basis, the first ECL ‘switch-on’ state was achieved. The ECL intensity declined remarkably upon the introduction of AuNPs-Apt owing to the ECL-RET from the Ru@SiO2-NGQDs to AuNPs, thereby achieving the ‘switch-off’ state (curve b). After incubation with BSA to avoid the nonspecific binding effect, the ECL signal of the resultant electrode decreased even further (curve c). Finally, recovery of the ECL intensity was induced by Hg2+, which bound with its aptamer to form the T-Hg2+-T structure and release AuNPs from the sensing interface (curves d-f). Therefore, the ECL-RET process was terminated and the second ‘switch-on’ state was achieved. Additionally, it can be observed from curves d-f, as the Hg2+ concentration increased from 1 × 10−8 M to 1 × 10−6 M, the ECL intensity was gradually enhanced, verifying the feasibility of the proposed ‘on–off-on’-type ECL aptasensor for Hg2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance investigation of the ‘on–off-on’ ECL aptasensor for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the proposed ‘on–off-on’ ECL aptasensor for Hg2+ detection was investigated under the optimal experimental conditions. (The optimisation of the experimental conditions is presented in Fig. S7). Fig. 5 A shows that the recovered ECL intensity increased gradually as the concentration of Hg2+ increased from 0 to 1 × 10−6 M, suggesting the more T-Hg2+-T structures were generated and more AuNPs were released from the GCE surface. As displayed in the inset of Fig. 5A, the ECL intensity (I ECL) was linearly correlated with the logarithm of Hg2+ concentration (lgc Hg 2+) in the range of 1 × 10−14 to 1 × 10−6 M. The corresponding calibration equation was I ECL = 255lgc Hg 2+ + 4490 and the correlation coefficient (R 2) was 0.9978. The limit of detection (LOD) was measured as 3.0 × 10−15 M according to a previously reported method based on a signal-to-noise ratio (I Signal/I Noise) of 3 [6,45]. The linear range and LOD of the proposed method for Hg2+ detection were compared with other ECL sensors based on different signal output modes (Table 1 ). Significantly, the present ‘on–off-on’ ECL aptasensor provided the widest working range with an order of magnitude of eight and a lower LOD than almost all the reported ECL methods. These features arose from the improved sensitivity enabled by the dual amplification strategy of a self-enhanced luminophore and ECL-RET.

The selectivity of the developed ECL aptasensor for Hg2+ detection was evaluated by investigating the ECL response of the sensor before and after incubation with different interfering ions (Mg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, K+, Fe3+, Na+, Cd2+) at 50-fold higher concentrations than that of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 5B, the change in ECL intensity caused by individual interfering ions or their mixtures was nearly negligible (columns 1–11). In contrast, the addition of Hg2+ into the sensing system resulted in an obvious ECL signal recovery (column 12). These results revealed that the developed aptasensor could selectively detect Hg2+ as was further verified by the ECL response induced by a mixture of interfering ions and Hg2+ (column 13).

The repeatability of the developed ECL aptasensor was assessed by comparing the ECL intensity of six groups of aptasensors fabricated under the same conditions. Fig. 5C shows that the six aptasensors exhibited nearly the same ECL response with an average intensity of 2686 a.u. and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.13%, indicating good repeatability of the developed aptasensor. Finally, recording the ECL signal of a specified aptasensor for 15 days showed that the ECL signal hardly decreased, with the ECL intensity remaining at 98% of the initial value (Fig. 5D). These results demonstrated that the proposed ECL aptasensor could remain stable during relatively long application duration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of the proposed method for Hg2+detection in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the proposed method for the detection of Hg2+ in real samples was assessed by the standard addition method. As shown in Table S1, no Hg2+ was detected in the tap water. With the addition of 1 × 10−9, 1 × 10−8 or 1 × 10−7 M Hg2+, the Hg2+ concentrations in the corresponding spiked solutions were measured as 0.94 × 10−9, 1.04 × 10−8 and 0.97 × 10−7 M. The recoveries were between 94% and 104% with RSDs of 1.3–3.5%. Similar results are obtained when the method was applied to detect Hg2+ in canal water, where the recoveries ranged from 92% to 113% and the RSDs were below 5.8%. These results suggested that the proposed aptasensor could be feasibly applied for Hg2+ assay in actual water samples.

In summary, enabled by the combination of the self-enhanced ECL behaviour of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs and ECL-RET between Ru@SiO2-NGQDs and AuNPs, an ‘on–off-on’ model ECL aptasensor was designed to detect Hg2+. The Ru@SiO2-NGQDs provided the first ‘switch-on’ state, and AuNPs exhibited good quenching efficiency for the ECL of Ru@SiO2-NGQDs, leading to a low background signal (the ‘switch-off’ state). The high intensity of the first ‘switch-on’ signal and the low ‘switch-off’ signal guaranteed the sensitivity of the ‘on–off-on’ ECL system. As a result, upon the addition of Hg2+, amplified ECL recovery efficiency (the second ‘switch-on’ state) was obtained. On this basis, the proposed ECL sensing method presented excellent analytical performances for Hg2+ assay. Additionally, the developed aptasensor was successfully applied to detect Hg2+ in real water samples. The proposed novel dual signal amplification strategy can broaden the application of the ECL method in analytical sensing.

Libo Li: Methodology, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Bainian Chen: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiaohong Liu: Investigation, Software, Writing – review & editing. Panao Jiang: Investigation, Data curation, Software, Writing – review & editing. Lijun Luo: Investigation, Resources, Software. Xia Li: Investigation, Resources, Software. Tianyan You: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61801195 and 22074055), the Innovation/Entrepreneurship Program of Jiangsu Province and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD-2018-87), a Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (KJ2018BZC043) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2019MB068).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2022.116063.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
